entry.title: Re: Does SAP JCo hold memory if all heavy logic is in ABA...
entry.content:
The answers are:

No. No. And you should run a Java memory profiler to find where exactly the memory is allocated, and then take it from there. A sudden spike doesn't sound like a memory leak. (If there was a leak, e.g. in your application, in JCo or in another third-party library, then memory would more likely be slowly increasing over time.)

SAP JVM 8 has some very good profiling tools included. For example, you could start $JAVA_HOME/bin/jvmmon-gui and then attach to your running Java program and watch the memory allocation.

entry.title: The SAP Cell and Gene Therapy Consortium in NYC – Insights, Innovations, and a Warm Invitation!
entry.content:
Last week, SAP hosted another exciting edition of the SAP Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration (CGTO) Consortium in vibrant New York City. I vividly remember how the first such event, just two years ago, featured just my colleague Mandar Paralkar, me, and three customer participants. That’s why it was fantastic and heartwarming to witness the energy, curiosity, and collaborative spirit between the 80+ participants that filled the 52nd floor of our Hudson Yards office. As always, NYC, a city renowned for its diversity and dynamism, provided the perfect backdrop to bring together leaders from SAP, top life sciences companies, and our strategic partners for this important endeavor.

 

 

During two inspiring days, we dived deep into the heart of the CGT industry’s most pressing challenges and explored innovative solutions that are shaping the future. Our agenda was rich with insightful presentations from industry experts that sparked many good discussions:

  • Sumitomo Pharma’s Himanshu Mishra shared valuable insights into their unique patient and product journey. Having both, features of an allogeneic as well as an autologous product and employing production orders on the ERP, Himanshu explained how working with us as the SAP CGTO product team helped him and SAP both further evolve the solution approach.
  • Beam Therapeutics’ Vishal Khanderia shared his lessons learned from his enormous experience of 20+ years of managing clinical & commercial supply chains across multiple modalities and product launches and sparked meaningful conversations around cutting-edge approaches.
  • Roche/Genentech’s Tour Ala highlighted the critical role and complexity of effective treatment scheduling. With Roche being the initial co-innovation partner for SAP’s CGTO, Tour jokingly introduced himself as the “grandfather of CGTO” and in his very own approach tried to elicit a view from the audience on how interested they are in governing standards for CGT solutions.

Our very own SAP CGTO product team, including Monika Birdi (Product Manager), Arvind Ganju (Chief Product Lead), Mandar Paralkar (VP Life Science), Sachin Kohli (Product Manager), and Sagar Nanda (Product Manager), took attendees on a tour of exciting product updates, strategic enhancements, and insightful demos with Arvind’s presentation of the SAP CGTO roadmap for the years 2025 and 2026 as a special focus topic. I think it is safe to say that we have received an enthusiastic validation from attendees for our roadmap direction, with excitement around topics like C(D)MO collaboration and the complexities of treatment scheduling.

I said it before and I will say it again, SAP would not be where it is without the incredible ecosystem and all the support we get from our partners! We are grateful for our partners that not only showed up and contributed to the discussions but brought their complimentary solution and service offerings that were showcased at the partner demo-stations for all the participants to learn more about. This is why we can confidently approach any challenge knowing that if there is any “white-space” left that we cannot address directly, our partners will take it up and deliver a high-quality solution for our customers.

One especially pleasant session for me was our fireside chat moderated by SAP’s Mazahir Valikarimwala and featuring Immatic’s Pinar Cicalese, Tenthpin’s Philipp Suek, and me as one of the SAP CGTO product managers. It highlighted Immatic’s journey from pre-clinical to clinical and hopefully soon to commercial stage. Pinar also explained their vision for digitization and their expectations and goals for the ongoing projects working closely with SAP. Philipp used this opportunity to showcase the groundbreaking Tenthpin Treatment Order Portal (T/TOP), the first healthcare provider-facing ordering portal for CGT products built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)—the same robust platform that also powers SAP CGTO.

 

We all came back energized and excited reflecting on the fruitful exchanges, many insights, and a lot of fun that we had with our customers and partners. I was very happy to see that our effort paid off and people felt it was time well spent as the feedback from the event was incredibly positive, with attendees praising the substantial progress and clear vision outlined for the near future. Participants appreciated the transparent dialogue, interactive nature, and invaluable opportunity to exchange ideas and challenges with their industry peers and us as the SAP CGTO product team. We definitely appreciate it!

Looking ahead, we warmly invite you to be part of this vibrant consortium. Our next SAP CGT Consortium event is coming up in October (more details coming soon!). If you're interested in shaping the future of SAP CGTO, discussing critical industry challenges, and connecting with other leaders in cell and gene therapy, please reach out and join us!

Want to see SAP CGTO in action or discuss how it can support your goals? Reach out today—we'd be delighted to arrange a meeting.

See you in October!

entry.title: Re: How to remove pacemaker without impacting Application...
entry.content:
Question 1 - How we can remove the cluster configuration like resource agents, resource groups etc. without impacting the SAP application.

Answer : 

 1.1 Below documents can be referred to destroy the cluster configuration : 

     - How to remove/delete/destroy the entire Cluster configuration for pacemaker cluster? 
       https://access.redhat.com/solutions/45916

       **NOTE: Removing Pacemaker from the server will disable cluster based resource management, meaning all resources (including SAPInstance,Filesystem, ASCS, and ERS) previously managed by the cluster will be stopped. After removal, you must manually handle tasks such as mounting required file systems and starting/stopping SAP instances. 


  1.2 Additional Information on Upgrading SAP environments from RHEL 7 to RHEL 8 :
  
      - https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux_for_sap_solutions/8/html-single/upgrading_sap_environments_from_rhel_7_to_rhel_8/asmb_supported-upgrade-paths_upgrading-7-to-8#proc_preparing-upgrade_asmb_upgrading-hana-system


-- 

Question 2: Maintaining both servers after removing the cluster configuration

  1. Standalone Configuration:

    • Configure 01 to run ASCS standalone. Ensure that all necessary SAP services are enabled and configured correctly on this server.

  2. Server Maintenance:

    • 02 can be repurposed or kept as a backup server. Ensure that it is properly maintained and updated, even if it is not actively running SAP services.

  3. Monitoring and Backup:

    • Implement monitoring solutions to keep track of the health and performance of 01. Regular backups should be scheduled to prevent data loss.

  4. Network and Security:

    • Ensure that both servers are properly secured and that network configurations are optimized for standalone operation.

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud for planning - Data Action based...
entry.content:
Hi @guenay 

To get your expected result, please refer to the Advanced formulas script. 

I assume that the Actual data(Cost & Quantities) is for 2024.Dec and Forecast data(Quantities) is for 2025.Jan.

MEMBERSET [d/Measures] = "Amount"

VARIABLEMEMBER #Agg_Cost OF [d/Account]
VARIABLEMEMBER #Agg_Quantity OF [d/Account]

FOREACH [d/Material].[p/Material_Group]
	DATA([d/Account] = #Agg_Cost, [d/Date] = "202412", [d/Material] = "#")
	= RESULTLOOKUP([d/Version] = "public.Actual", [d/Account] = "COST", [d/Date] = "202412")
	DATA([d/Account] = #Agg_Quantity, [d/Date] = "202412", [d/Material] = "#")
	= RESULTLOOKUP([d/Version] = "public.Actual", [d/Account] = "Quantities", [d/Date] = "202412")

	DATA([d/Account] = "Revenue", [d/Date] = "202501")
	= RESULTLOOKUP([d/Account] = #Agg_Cost, [d/Date] = "202412", [d/Material] = "#") 
	/ RESULTLOOKUP([d/Account] = #Agg_Quantity, [d/Date] = "202412", [d/Material] = "#")
	* RESULTLOOKUP([d/Account] = "Quantities", [d/Date] = "202501")
ENDFOR

To calculate the average cost rate per Material Group, the variable member (#Agg_Cost, #Agg_Quantity) has been defined, and the value of the Cost & Quantity is aggregated by Material group. The Forecast revenue is calculated by aggregating the Cost & Quantity by material group. 

 

I hope this is helpful to you, and if I have misunderstood anything, please feel free to reach out.

Moonjun

entry.title: Simple UI Applications with Application Frontend Service
entry.content:
In the ever-evolving world of web UI applications, managed SaaS solutions are the preferred choice for hosting and serving UI applications. They offer efficiency and cost-effectiveness. SAP BTP provides robust solutions for UI applications from the early days of its cloud. The latest offering, Application Frontend service, combines the best legacy features with new advancements in technology and infrastructure. In this post I’ll share with you how to get started with Application Frontend service using the CLI, but MTA and API are supported as well.

Prerequisites

To proceed with this tutorial and try everything on your own, you’ll need a few things

  • An SAP BTP subaccount, where you have administrator privileges, with
    • An entitlement to use the “build-default” of Application Frontend service
    • Trust with SAP Cloud Identity tenant
  • Node.js v22.6.0 or later installed on your development environment
  • Application Frontend service CLI tool—afctl

To install the afctl, run

npm install -g @sap/appfront-cli

Setup

To start working with Application Frontend service, you need to subscribe to it and grant yourself the permissions to use it.

  1. In the SAP BTP cockpit, in the subaccount, go to Services > Instances and Subscriptions. Then create a subscription the “build-default” of Application Frontend service.
  2. In the subaccount, go to Security > Role Collections. Then create a role collection (for example, Application Frontend Developer). Open the newly created role collection and add yourself as a user. Make sure the user is from an “application users” identity provider. Add the “Application_Frontend_Developer” role to the role collection and save.

Development & Deployment

Let’s develop an application that prints “Hello, <NAME>!”, where the <NAME> is the first name of the logged-in user. We’ll use Application Frontend’s built-in current user REST API, which returns the attributes of the logged-in user as JSON.

Application Frontend service is agnostic to the tools, development model, and UI frameworks that are used to develop UI applications. However, some application configuration files are required:

The manifest.json file should, at a minimum, declare application name and version in the following format:

{
    "sap.app": {
        "id": "hello",
        "applicationVersion": {
            "version": "1.0.0"
        }
    }
}

The xs-app.json file, where the desired web server features, such as, the routing, the welcome file and authentication are configured. Some features, for example the authentication are enabled by default, and you have an option to switch them off in this file:

{
    "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
    "routes": [{
        "source": "^/user-api/currentUser$",
        "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    }, {
        "source": ".*",
        "service": "app-front"
    }]
}

With this example we configure 3 things:

  • If no specific path to the resource is present in the URL, send a redirect to /index.html
  • When the URL path is exactly /user-api/currentUser, send the JSON response with logged-in user attributes
  • For any other URL path, use the URL path as path to the static resource within deployed application

The index.html file would be the following:

<html>
<head>
    <style>
        body {
            display: grid;
            place-content: center;
            position: absolute;
            inset: 0;
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Loading...</h1>
    <script>
        fetch(
            './user-api/currentUser', 
            {credentials: 'include'}
        )
        .then(res => res.json())
        .then(user => {
            const h1 = document.querySelector('h1');
            h1.textContent = `Hello, ${user.firstname}!`;
        });
    </script>
</body>
</html>

To deploy the application, use afctl. The first thing, you need to do is to log in:

afctl login --sso -a <api_endpoint>

You can grab the <api_endpoint> from the Application Frontend service landing page. To open it, in the subaccount in the SAP BTP Cockpit, go to Services > Instances and Subscriptions, and click on the Application Frontend service link.

Now, let’s open the terminal in the root folder of our application containing these 3 files and deploy UI application, using afctl:

afctl push

After a few seconds, you should see the runtime URL of deployed application.

Open the link in the browser, log in with your SAP Cloud Identity (if you are not already logged in) and you’ll see “Hello, <NAME>!”, where <NAME> is the first name of the logged in user, in the middle of the web page.

That’s it! Very simple. No mess with infrastructure, servers, certificates, etc. Developing and deploying subsequent applications will be even simpler, since you already have everything set up.

What’s Next

In this post we covered only the very basics of Application Frontend service. In the upcoming posts from the series, we’ll take a look at more advanced Application Frontend capabilities, including:

  • Accessing back-end systems
  • Setting up authorization
  • Securing UI application (CSRF, click-jacking, verb tampering, CSP, CORS)
  • Developing and deploying full-stack applications (MTA)
  • Managing application versions
  • Local preview
  • Integration with SAP BTP services and tools (SAP BTP cockpit, Business Application Studio, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Fiori tools)

Stay tuned to learn more about what Application Frontend service has to offer already now and the future features to come. Happy coding!

entry.title: Unexpected Exchange Rate Difference and Zero-Balance Clearing Entries During Payment Clearing
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I'm encountering an issue while clearing a customer open item in foreign currency (SGD). The original invoice was posted at an exchange rate of 1 SGD = 20,000 VND, totaling 612,000 VND (equivalent to 30.60 SGD). The customer payment was received at a different exchange rate, resulting in a payment amount of 29.38 SGD.

Upon clearing the open item, SAP generated the following journal entries:

i have some questions here:

  • Why is there both an exchange gain and loss recorded in this clearing?

 

  • Why is the exchange gain (29.06) and loss (30.28) split this way, while the actual payment received was 29.38?
    The difference between gain/loss and the actual payment doesn't reconcile intuitively.

  • Where does the 0.32 SGD figure come from?
    Why is it not part of the exchange gain or loss but instead sent to the Zero Balance Clearing account?

  • Why doesn’t the system record the full exchange rate difference (between 30.60 and 29.38) as either gain or loss?

  • Could this behavior be influenced by document splitting, rounding logic, or exchange rate type configuration?

    Any insight would be greatly appreciated.

    Thank you,
    Andromeda

entry.title: Re: Adjustment to the provisioning script (IPS Job)

Hi @yogananda, 

I would like to describe it more precisely. Currently, I am provisioning from EntraID towards IAS. Only 20 users from a group are needed. The job still reads all 20,000 users and then only creates/updates the 20 users. My idea was whether there is a way to first read the members of a group and then only create those members. This way, I have reduced the traffic from reading 20,000 users to reading 20 users. I hope I was able to describe it well.

BR

btpnerd

entry.title: Re: Adopting General Display Name
entry.content:
Hi@katrien1 ,

If general display name is enabled, it is not possible to see different name in profile header and global search. The purpose of general display name is to unify the display of employee name. There are some cases employee's first name + last name is still used due to legal requirement or admin purpose. You can refer to https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/implementing-and-using-diversity-and-inclusion-features-in-sap-successfactors/important-notes-about-general-display-name-adoption for list of the features. 

For name format in legal entity, as I know 

"Name Format for Legal Entity" is configured for formal name of EC personal information

"Name Format for General Display" is configured for general display name 

I don't know what is the purpose of other name format, it may be customized locally. If you don't need them you may re-configure it as documented in https://me.sap.com/notes/2435013/E

Thanks

Harvey

entry.title: Time off - Italy best practice
entry.content:
Hello,

Our client has the following requirement: 

for holidays they need three counters:
1) current vacation
2) Year-1 vacation 
3) Year-2 vacation
The year-1 and year-2 counters should be filled with the remaining balance and bookable date from the "previuos" counter.
ES: the current vacation is for the year 2025 with a bookable date of 30/06/2027 and a balance of 22 days.
On 1 January 2026, a new current vacation for 2026 needs to be created and the remaining balances related to 2025 and its bookable date need to be moved to the vacation counter of year 1,  and so on for other counters:

Is there a way to manage this requirement using time off?
We download the best practice for Italy, but the best practice manages only two counters (ITA VACATION and ITA PREVIOUS VACATION) and this configuration doesn't cover our needs.

Thank you. 

Regards

HCM Time Management

entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,


We need to check which T-Codes or Fiori tiles were used by user in the last 2 months.

Please share the steps or guidance as soon as possible.


Regards,


Udayraj K


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to Check User T-Code/Tile Usage (Last 2 Months)
entry.content:
Hi

Kindly refer the below KBA

3212871 - How to get "Identity and Access Management" information - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

You can use the "I_IAMBusinessUserLogonDetails" view to check Business User Logon Details.

While it is not possible to track which user accessed a specific app, you can monitor the applications that business users are authorized to access in the S/4HANA Cloud system using the IAM Information System app

Steps to follow:

  1. Set the "Main Entity" as "Application"
  2. Click on "Application - Business User" tab
  3. Search with the business user's name
  4. You can get the list of applications which are used by this business user

You can also find more details on SAP Help Portal - IAM Information System.

entry.title: Re: Connecting a remote SQL Anywhereto an system SAP usin...
entry.content:
Basically, there are two ways to do it:

  1. Define the SQL Anywhere as a "secondary database" in the DB setup of the SAP System.
    The people who know how to do this, would be the DB Interface team, but there is no tag for this component in SAP Community?! I have added the "Basis Technology" tag to this question. Perhaps someone from that area can provide more details on how secondary databases work.
    (Or you could search SAP Help with the keyword "secondary database".)

  2. The following may not be the best, if you want to transfer huge tables from the SQL Anywhere DB to ABAP, but if you just want to make some standard SELECTs and get the result set back into your ABAP program, the following would be a quite easy possibility:
    1. Install SAP BC (either on the same host, where the DB runs, or on one of the SAP System app-servers)
      Download link: https://support.sap.com/sbc-download
      Documentation: ​https://support.sap.com/en/product/connectors/bc/details.html?anchorId=section_295234589
    2. Define an RFC Listener for the SAP System
      (Described in the SAPAdapterGuide, chapter 4)
    3. Get a JDBC driver for SQL Anywhere and define a DB Alias for this DB
      (Described in the AdministrationGuide, chapter 14)
    4. Optionally: if there is a small number of predefined SQL statements or stored procedures, that you want to execute on the DB, you can generate "Services" that execute precisely one SQL statement/stored procedure. Otherwise you can use the "execSQL Service", which takes a generic SQL statement as input and executes it on the DB. Then you will be able to execute any SQL statement from your ABAP program, but you'll have to be careful about SQL injection.
      (See also AdministrationGuide, chapter 14)
    5. In ABAP, define one or more RFC-enabled function modules, that have a suitable table parameter for holding the result set of such an SQL statement. (Or "mis-use" existing function modules: for example, if the USERLIST table of BAPI_USER_GETLIST has a suitable layout for holding the result set of your SQL statement, then you can simply use BAPI_USER_GETLIST for fetching data from your DB... 🙂 )
    6. In the Business Connector, define Inbound Maps for these function modules to the Services created in step 4.
      (See also SAPAdapterGuide, chapter 4)
    7. Now in your ABAP program, you can get an internal table with the result set from your SQL Anywhere by simply calling
      CALL FUNCTION 'XYZ' DESTINATION 'SAP_BC

      This may look like a long list, but each step only takes a few minutes, so a basic proof of concept can probably be done in less than an hour.
